narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is the threshold we use to make these tough admission decisions. Your profile shows strong commitment as a full-time student and comes from a lower-middle family income background, but it also includes some academic hurdles like being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an LSAT score of 34, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.60. Compared to typical applicants—who often rank around the middle of their class in those early years, score about 36.7 on the LSAT, and have a GPA near 3.21—your metrics fell notably below those benchmarks. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your first-year decile ranking, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar exam struggle more than any other factor. Right behind that was your third-year decile, also contributing in the same direction by signaling ongoing academic challenges. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, similarly nudging the model toward a failure prediction due to being lower than average. Other elements like your Asian racial background—which is less common than the majority White applicants at about 84%—and your family income level came next, each adding a bit more weight toward the unfavorable outcome. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, reinforcing the pattern with its below-average standing, while your status as a female applicant and full-time enrollment had the least sway, though still slightly toward failure. Overall, these features together painted a picture riskier than the typical successful student's profile for bar passage. While demographics like gender, race, and family income aren't things you can change, you can take realistic steps to strengthen your candidacy moving forward. Consider retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, as that's a key lever still within reach. Building a stronger academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs could help offset those early deciles and GPA. With focused preparation, many students in similar spots turn things around and boost their bar exam odds significantly. Keep pushing—you've got the dedication as a full-time aspiring lawyer, and targeted improvements can open doors next time.
